title: How to stop languishing and start finding flow
summary:
1
00:00:00,288 --> 00:00:02,749
Vous avez tous de longues listes de choses à faire.

2
00:00:02,790 --> 00:00:07,420
Comme je déteste perdre mon temps, j’ai une liste de choses à ne pas faire.

3
00:00:07,462 --> 00:00:09,255
Ne pas parcourir les réseaux sociaux,

4
00:00:09,380 --> 00:00:11,257
ne pas regarder mon téléphone au lit

5
00:00:11,299 --> 00:00:12,800
et ne pas allumer la télé

6
00:00:12,842 --> 00:00:15,678
si je ne sais pas ce que je vais regarder.

7
00:00:15,720 --> 00:00:19,057
Mais l’an dernier, j’ai enfreint toutes ces règles.

8
00:00:19,098 --> 00:00:22,351
Je restais à regarder bien après minuit, des trucs lugubres,

9
00:00:22,351 --> 00:00:24,979
à jouer des jeux interminables de Scrabble

10
00:00:24,979 --> 00:00:29,776
et à me gaver de séries télé qui n’étaient même pas intéressantes.

11
00:00:30,610 --> 00:00:33,488
Le lendemain, je me réveillais dans le brouillard et jurais,

12
00:00:33,488 --> 00:00:35,573
« Ce soir, au lit à 22 h. »

13
00:00:35,573 --> 00:00:39,118
Mais je continuais soir après soir, semaine après semaine.

14
00:00:39,160 --> 00:00:40,411
Qu’est-ce qui m’arrivait ?

15
00:00:41,079 --> 00:00:42,830
Je suis psychologue organisationnel,

16
00:00:42,830 --> 00:00:45,625
j’ai passé toute ma carrière à étudier la motivation.

17
00:00:45,666 --> 00:00:50,463
Ça m’agace quand je ne peux pas expliquer mon propre comportement.

18
00:00:50,505 --> 00:00:53,049
Je ne déprimais pas. J’avais de l’espoir.

19
00:00:53,049 --> 00:00:55,593
Pas de surmenage, j’avais de l’énergie.

20
00:00:55,635 --> 00:00:58,429
Je n’étais pas seul, j’avais de la famille.

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:03,017
Je me sentais juste un peu désœuvré et un peu triste.

22
00:01:03,017 --> 00:01:06,020
Finalement, je me suis souvenu qu’il y avait un mot pour ça :

23
00:01:06,020 --> 00:01:07,396
la langueur.

24
00:01:07,396 --> 00:01:11,234
Langueur dans le sens de vide, de stagnation et d’ennui.

25
00:01:11,234 --> 00:01:13,778
Ce terme a été décrit par le sociologue Corey Keyes

26
00:01:13,778 --> 00:01:16,989
et immortalisé par la philosophe Mariah Carey.

27
00:01:16,989 --> 00:01:18,991
(Rires)

28
00:01:19,033 --> 00:01:21,953
Se languir, c’est avoir l’impression de patauger,

29
00:01:21,953 --> 00:01:25,164
de regarder sa vie à travers un parebrise embué.

30
00:01:25,206 --> 00:01:29,168
Combien d’entre vous ont ressenti ça ces derniers mois ?

31
00:01:31,379 --> 00:01:34,507
Ceux qui n’ont pas eu l’énergie de lever la main...

32
00:01:34,549 --> 00:01:35,675
(Rires)

33
00:01:35,716 --> 00:01:38,469
vous vous languissez probablement en ce moment même.

34
00:01:38,511 --> 00:01:41,722
Et vous, là, qui n’avez pas ri, vous vous languissez, c’est sûr.

35
00:01:43,099 --> 00:01:44,267
Bizarement...

36
00:01:44,350 --> 00:01:46,561
[Comment vous sentez-vous aujourd’hui ? Bof.]

37
00:01:46,602 --> 00:01:47,603
(Rires)

38
00:01:47,603 --> 00:01:49,021
Certains ont passé le test.

39
00:01:49,939 --> 00:01:55,069
Bizarrement, ce qui m’a sauvé de cette langueur, c’est Mario Kart.

40
00:01:56,028 --> 00:01:57,530
Revenons un peu en arrière.

41
00:01:58,322 --> 00:01:59,740
Au début de la Covid,

42
00:01:59,740 --> 00:02:03,911
beaucoup d’entre nous luttaient contre la peur, le chagrin et l’isolement.

43
00:02:03,953 --> 00:02:06,873
Avec la pandémie qui n’en finissait pas,

44
00:02:06,914 --> 00:02:10,209
notre langueur aigüe s’est transformée en langueur chronique.

45
00:02:10,251 --> 00:02:12,170
On vivait tous « Un jour sans fin ».

46
00:02:12,211 --> 00:02:14,964
C’était comme si le monde entier stagnait.

47
00:02:15,006 --> 00:02:18,509
J’ai donc écrit un article sur la langueur,

48
00:02:18,551 --> 00:02:22,054
« l’enfant oubliée de la santé mentale ».

49
00:02:22,096 --> 00:02:25,892
et j’ai suggéré qu’elle était sans doute l’émotion dominante de notre époque.

50
00:02:25,933 --> 00:02:27,852
Tout à coup, on ne parlait que de ça.

51
00:02:27,852 --> 00:02:29,604
Elle était dans tous les médias.

52
00:02:29,604 --> 00:02:32,356
Les gens célèbres, la famille royale en parlaient.

53
00:02:32,356 --> 00:02:34,650
Je n’ai jamais vu autant de gens enthousiastes

54
00:02:34,650 --> 00:02:37,278
parler de leur manque total d’enthousiasme.

55
00:02:37,278 --> 00:02:40,198
(Rires)

56
00:02:40,198 --> 00:02:41,449
Et...

57
00:02:42,366 --> 00:02:43,910
je pense...

58
00:02:43,951 --> 00:02:45,870
que nommer la langueur

59
00:02:45,912 --> 00:02:49,207
a aidé les gens à donner un sens à leurs expériences déconcertantes.

60
00:02:49,248 --> 00:02:51,167
Pourquoi, même après avoir été vaccinés,

61
00:02:51,167 --> 00:02:54,462
les gens avaient-ils du mal à se réjouir du reste de l’année ?

62
00:02:54,503 --> 00:02:57,173
Quand « Le Trésor des Templiers » est passé à la télé,

63
00:02:57,215 --> 00:03:00,468
pourquoi ma femme connaissait-elle déjà le film par cœur ?

64
00:03:00,509 --> 00:03:03,471
Et pourquoi je me couchais tard,

65
00:03:03,512 --> 00:03:06,891
victime de la procrastination de vengeance à l’heure du coucher ?

66
00:03:07,016 --> 00:03:08,768
(Rires)

67
00:03:08,893 --> 00:03:12,855
Il n’est que 23 h, j’ai tout le temps de gâcher demain en restant éveillé

68
00:03:13,272 --> 00:03:16,192
à regarder n’importe quoi sur internet.

69
00:03:17,693 --> 00:03:22,949
On cherchait la joie dans un jour morne et un but dans une pandémie sans fin.

70
00:03:23,908 --> 00:03:26,619
Mais la langueur n’est pas spécifique à une pandémie.

71
00:03:26,661 --> 00:03:28,663
Elle fait partie de la condition humaine.

72
00:03:28,663 --> 00:03:32,250
Vingt ans de recherches ont montré que la langueur perturbe la concentration

73
00:03:32,291 --> 00:03:34,210
et plombe la motivation.

74
00:03:34,210 --> 00:03:36,671
C’est aussi un facteur de risque pour la dépression

75
00:03:36,712 --> 00:03:39,840
parce que la langueur passe inaperçue.

76
00:03:39,882 --> 00:03:42,426
Vous ne réalisez pas que votre motivation s’effrite

77
00:03:42,468 --> 00:03:44,595
ou que votre plaisir s’émousse.

78
00:03:44,637 --> 00:03:47,598
Vous êtes indifférent à votre propre indifférence,

79
00:03:47,640 --> 00:03:49,433
donc, vous ne cherchez pas d’aide

80
00:03:49,433 --> 00:03:53,646
et vous ne faites sans doute rien pour vous aider vous-même.

81
00:03:53,646 --> 00:03:54,897
Bof.

82
00:03:56,065 --> 00:03:59,235
La langueur est non seulement difficile à détecter,

83
00:03:59,235 --> 00:04:02,571
mais dans certaines cultures, il est difficile d’en parler.

84
00:04:02,571 --> 00:04:04,448
Quand les gens demandent, « Ça va ? »,

85
00:04:04,448 --> 00:04:06,659
on est censés répondre, « Super ! »

86
00:04:06,659 --> 00:04:09,036
ou « Je m’éclate ! »

87
00:04:09,954 --> 00:04:12,123
C’est ce qu’on appelle la positivité toxique.

88
00:04:12,164 --> 00:04:14,292
(Rires)

89
00:04:16,919 --> 00:04:23,217
C’est se sentir obligé d’être positif à tout prix, tout le temps.

90
00:04:23,259 --> 00:04:25,803
Si vous répondez, « Comme si comme ça »,

91
00:04:25,845 --> 00:04:29,098
les gens vont vous encourager à voir le bon côté des choses

92
00:04:29,098 --> 00:04:31,309
ou à apprécier ce que vous avez.

93
00:04:31,309 --> 00:04:32,935
Ce qui est non seulement irritant,

94
00:04:32,935 --> 00:04:35,021
mais peut même être de mauvais conseil.

95
00:04:35,021 --> 00:04:36,772
J’aimerais deux volontaires.

96
00:04:37,606 --> 00:04:40,318
Vous serez désignés s’il le faut. Ne vous précipitez pas.

97
00:04:40,318 --> 00:04:41,902
Bien, en voilà un.

98
00:04:41,902 --> 00:04:43,195
Venez près d’un micro.

99
00:04:43,195 --> 00:04:46,991
Et quelqu’un d’autre, oui, là-bas. Allez près de ce micro.

100
00:04:46,991 --> 00:04:49,035
Applaudissons nos deux volontaires.

101
00:04:49,076 --> 00:04:50,870
(Applaudissements)

102
00:04:52,872 --> 00:04:54,373
Bonjour. Quel est votre nom ?

103
00:04:54,415 --> 00:04:55,458
Martin.

104
00:04:55,458 --> 00:04:56,584
Merci.

105
00:04:56,584 --> 00:04:59,628
Pouvez-vous nommer trois bonnes choses dans votre vie ?

106
00:05:01,672 --> 00:05:06,177
Je suis marié, en bonne santé et heureux.

107
00:05:06,218 --> 00:05:08,929
Content que le mariage soit en première place. Bien joué.

108
00:05:09,055 --> 00:05:10,848
Et de ce côté. Quel est votre nom ?

109
00:05:10,890 --> 00:05:11,891
Lee.

110
00:05:11,891 --> 00:05:14,727
Lee, pouvez-vous nommer 42 bonnes choses dans votre vie ?

111
00:05:16,270 --> 00:05:19,398
Mon chat, Titchypoo, mon chien, Enzo,

112
00:05:19,398 --> 00:05:20,691
ma femme, Jazz.

113
00:05:20,691 --> 00:05:22,610
Troisième, derrière le chien et le chat.

114
00:05:22,651 --> 00:05:23,652
(Rires)

115
00:05:23,694 --> 00:05:25,071
Bravo.

116
00:05:25,071 --> 00:05:27,156
Mes enfants, Indio et Walter,

117
00:05:27,156 --> 00:05:28,866
l’équipe de foot Manchester United,

118
00:05:28,866 --> 00:05:30,659
mes amis, TED.

119
00:05:31,702 --> 00:05:33,496
TED en huitième place.

120
00:05:33,496 --> 00:05:35,581
TED est très haut placé.

121
00:05:35,623 --> 00:05:40,753
La poésie de C.S. Lewis, E.E. Cummings, Dylan Thomas.

122
00:05:40,795 --> 00:05:43,506
Vous voulez nommer tous les poètes que vous connaissez ?

123
00:05:43,506 --> 00:05:45,341
Bien Lee, merci beaucoup.

124
00:05:45,341 --> 00:05:46,842
On va s’arrêter là.

125
00:05:46,884 --> 00:05:48,886
Applaudissons-les. Merci beaucoup.

126
00:05:48,928 --> 00:05:50,638
(Applaudissements)

127
00:05:52,473 --> 00:05:56,435
J’ai longtemps pensé que des gens comme Lee

128
00:05:56,435 --> 00:05:59,105
seraient plus heureux que Martin.

129
00:05:59,105 --> 00:06:02,483
Quand j’ai mené cette expérience j’ai réalisé que c’était l’inverse.

130
00:06:02,483 --> 00:06:05,653
Les gens à qui on demande de nommer beaucoup de bonnes choses

131
00:06:05,736 --> 00:06:08,072
sont en général moins heureux.

132
00:06:08,155 --> 00:06:10,950
Car ils tombent à court de raisons d’être optimistes.

133
00:06:10,950 --> 00:06:13,244
Et si vous ne connaissez pas autant de poètes…

134
00:06:13,285 --> 00:06:14,995
(Rires)

135
00:06:15,037 --> 00:06:18,249
Plus on cherche ce qu’il y a de bien dans notre vie,

136
00:06:18,290 --> 00:06:23,087
plus on a l’impression que notre vie n’est pas si bien que ça.

137
00:06:23,129 --> 00:06:24,755
Au début de la pandémie,

138
00:06:24,797 --> 00:06:27,758
des chercheurs ont trouvé que le meilleur signe de bien-être

139
00:06:27,800 --> 00:06:30,928
n’était pas l’optimisme, mais le flow.

140
00:06:31,971 --> 00:06:34,640
Atteindre le flow, c’est être dans la zone,

141
00:06:34,682 --> 00:06:37,435
comme l’a expliqué le psychologue Mihály Csíkszentmihályi.

142
00:06:38,144 --> 00:06:40,813
C’est être complètement plongé dans une activité.

143
00:06:40,813 --> 00:06:45,484
Ça peut être cuisiner, courir ou jardiner.

144
00:06:45,484 --> 00:06:46,986
On perd la notion de temps,

145
00:06:46,986 --> 00:06:50,364
ou peut-être même la notion de soi-même.

146
00:06:50,364 --> 00:06:52,992
Le flow c’est comme un attrait frénétique pour Netflix.

147
00:06:52,992 --> 00:06:55,327
Car on est transporté dans un autre monde,

148
00:06:55,369 --> 00:06:57,163
plongé dans une histoire.

149
00:06:57,204 --> 00:07:02,084
Mais la frénésie est une échappatoire temporaire à la langueur, pas un remède.

150
00:07:02,126 --> 00:07:05,921
Au mieux, vous forgez des relations asymétriques.

151
00:07:05,963 --> 00:07:08,549
Vous aimez sans doute traîner avec vos amis :

152
00:07:08,591 --> 00:07:10,342
Chandler,

153
00:07:10,384 --> 00:07:11,635
Arya,

154
00:07:11,677 --> 00:07:12,970
Dwight,

155
00:07:13,012 --> 00:07:14,388
Buffy ? Personne ?

156
00:07:14,430 --> 00:07:15,473
(Rires)

157
00:07:15,514 --> 00:07:16,765
Joe Exotic,

158
00:07:17,766 --> 00:07:18,976
Peppa Pig.

159
00:07:19,643 --> 00:07:21,896
Mais ils ne savent pas que vous existez.

160
00:07:23,814 --> 00:07:27,693
La frénésie est une action passive dans un monde fictif.

161
00:07:27,693 --> 00:07:30,571
Le flow optimal est une participation active

162
00:07:30,571 --> 00:07:32,323
dans le monde réel.

163
00:07:32,323 --> 00:07:35,659
C’est pour ça que ça m’a surpris de trouver mon flow,

164
00:07:35,701 --> 00:07:39,079
à conduire une voiture dans un jeu Nintendo.

165
00:07:41,040 --> 00:07:42,583
Au début de la pandémie,

166
00:07:42,625 --> 00:07:45,711
nos trois enfants avaient leurs cours en ligne, à la maison.

167
00:07:45,753 --> 00:07:47,922
Ça a duré un an.

168
00:07:47,963 --> 00:07:49,882
Ce n’était pas facile.

169
00:07:49,924 --> 00:07:52,718
On a trouvé ça sur le bulletin de notre enfant de six ans.

170
00:07:52,718 --> 00:07:57,515
[peut activer et désactiver le son tout seul quand on lui demande]

171
00:07:57,556 --> 00:07:59,183
(Rires)

172
00:08:00,017 --> 00:08:03,979
Je connais des adultes qui n’y sont pas encore parvenus,

173
00:08:03,979 --> 00:08:06,815
et pas juste en ligne, dans la vraie vie aussi.

174
00:08:06,815 --> 00:08:09,527
Je suppose que c’était donc une réussite à fêter.

175
00:08:09,527 --> 00:08:13,781
Comme beaucoup d’entre vous, nous étions séparés de notre grande famille.

176
00:08:13,781 --> 00:08:16,283
Ma sœur vivait à l’autre bout du pays.

177
00:08:16,283 --> 00:08:19,995
Un jour, nous nous sommes souvenus à quel point on adorait jouer à Mario Kart

178
00:08:20,037 --> 00:08:21,330
quand nous étions petits.

179
00:08:21,372 --> 00:08:24,458
Et ma sœur a dit, « On pourrait tous jouer en ligne.

180
00:08:24,500 --> 00:08:26,752
Et si on commençait un jeu en famille ? »

181
00:08:26,794 --> 00:08:30,756
Très vite, on s’est mis à jouer tous les jours en vidéoconférence.

182
00:08:31,632 --> 00:08:35,469
Après quelques semaines, j’ai arrêté de me sentir aussi bof.

183
00:08:35,511 --> 00:08:38,222
Je découvrais le zen dans l’art de jouer Mario Kart.

184
00:08:38,264 --> 00:08:39,515
(Rires)

185
00:08:39,557 --> 00:08:43,852
Dès leur réveil, les enfants demandaient à quelle heure on allait jouer.

186
00:08:43,852 --> 00:08:45,479
Ils étaient tout enthousiastes.

187
00:08:45,479 --> 00:08:48,774
Ils adoraient quand je me vantais d’une victoire imminente

188
00:08:48,774 --> 00:08:51,527
et que je me faisais exploser par une carapace bleue

189
00:08:51,527 --> 00:08:54,154
et regardais mes trois enfants me dépasser

190
00:08:54,154 --> 00:08:57,074
et passer la ligne d’arrivée dans leurs minuscules go-karts.

191
00:08:57,950 --> 00:09:01,412
On s’amusait tant qu’on a commencé une nouvelle tradition du samedi soir,

192
00:09:01,453 --> 00:09:03,080
une fois les enfants couchés.

193
00:09:03,122 --> 00:09:04,415
Mario Kart adultes.

194
00:09:04,456 --> 00:09:05,666
(Rires)

195
00:09:07,918 --> 00:09:09,962
Après avoir réfléchi à cette expérience,

196
00:09:10,004 --> 00:09:13,090
je suis fier de vous présenter pour la première fois

197
00:09:13,132 --> 00:09:15,926
ma théorie du flow optimal Mario Kart.

198
00:09:17,094 --> 00:09:21,974
Il faut trois conditions : la maîtrise, la pleine conscience et l’importance.

199
00:09:22,016 --> 00:09:23,726
Commençons par la maîtrise.

200
00:09:23,726 --> 00:09:24,852
La maîtrise est un truc

201
00:09:24,852 --> 00:09:27,855
que beaucoup d’entre nous ont eu du mal à trouver ces jours-ci.

202
00:09:27,855 --> 00:09:28,897
(Rires)

203
00:09:28,897 --> 00:09:31,191
Les psychologues suggèrent qu’au travail,

204
00:09:31,191 --> 00:09:34,278
le principal facteur de motivation et de joie

205
00:09:34,278 --> 00:09:36,030
est d’avoir le sentiment d’avancer.

206
00:09:36,947 --> 00:09:40,492
Dans la culture occidentale, il semble que notre bonheur dépende plus

207
00:09:40,492 --> 00:09:44,747
de l’état de nos projets aujourd’hui que de leur état hier.

208
00:09:44,788 --> 00:09:46,874
C’est pour ça que Nike dit, « Just Do It ».

209
00:09:47,958 --> 00:09:52,212
Si Nike avait débuté dans un pays tourné vers le passé comme la Chine,

210
00:09:52,254 --> 00:09:54,506
leur slogan aurait été, « Just Did It ».

211
00:09:57,176 --> 00:10:01,722
Si la langueur est synonyme de stagnation, le flow demande du dynamisme.

212
00:10:01,764 --> 00:10:05,017
Mais la maîtrise ne doit pas nécessairement être un exploit.

213
00:10:05,059 --> 00:10:07,102
Cela peut être de petites victoires.

214
00:10:07,102 --> 00:10:10,356
C’est ça qui m’attirait dans le Scrabble en ligne.

215
00:10:10,356 --> 00:10:13,525
La poussée d’adrénaline à trouver un mot de sept lettres.

216
00:10:13,525 --> 00:10:16,236
Ce sont ces petites victoires qui émoustillaient les gens

217
00:10:16,236 --> 00:10:18,947
qui contemplaient leur premier pain au levain fait maison.

218
00:10:18,947 --> 00:10:23,577
Cela explique aussi pourquoi un ingénieur a passé tout un après-midi

219
00:10:23,619 --> 00:10:28,374
à maîtriser l’art d’empiler des M&M’s les uns sur les autres.

220
00:10:28,415 --> 00:10:29,667
Regardez.

221
00:10:30,334 --> 00:10:32,711
(Vidéo) Ça va être plus dur que je ne pensais.

222
00:10:39,468 --> 00:10:40,761
Oh !

223
00:10:41,261 --> 00:10:42,721
Oh !

224
00:10:42,763 --> 00:10:44,598
Cinq M&Ms !

225
00:10:46,141 --> 00:10:47,351
(Rires)

226
00:10:48,560 --> 00:10:50,562
Ce fut un record du monde.

227
00:10:50,562 --> 00:10:52,147
(Rires)

228
00:10:52,147 --> 00:10:55,818
Ce genre de maîtrise dépend aussi de la deuxième condition du flow,

229
00:10:55,818 --> 00:10:57,111
la pleine conscience.

230
00:10:57,111 --> 00:11:00,447
Concentrer toute son attention sur une seule tâche.

231
00:11:00,447 --> 00:11:04,159
Quelque chose que peu d’entre nous font ces temps-ci.

232
00:11:04,159 --> 00:11:07,579
[Ça va ? Tu ne fais guère attention à ton livre, ton téléphone, la télé,

233
00:11:07,579 --> 00:11:10,833
ton ordi et les mots croisés que tu as commencé il y a dix minutes.]

234
00:11:10,833 --> 00:11:15,838
En moyenne, les gens vérifient leurs mails 74 fois pas jour,

235
00:11:15,921 --> 00:11:19,383
et changent d’activité toutes les dix minutes.

236
00:11:20,050 --> 00:11:22,886
Cela crée ce qu’on appelle les « time confetti »,

237
00:11:22,886 --> 00:11:25,889
où des moments qui pourraient être significatifs dans notre vie,

238
00:11:25,889 --> 00:11:27,141
on les déchire

239
00:11:27,182 --> 00:11:29,560
en morceaux de plus en plus petits et inutiles.

240
00:11:30,769 --> 00:11:34,815
Time confetti est l’ennemi du dynamisme et de l’excellence.

241
00:11:34,815 --> 00:11:37,609
Si vous voulez trouver le flow vous avez besoin de limites.

242
00:11:37,609 --> 00:11:40,821
[Pour ne pas regarder mon téléphone toutes les deux secondes.]

243
00:11:42,614 --> 00:11:43,907
(Rires)

244
00:11:45,492 --> 00:11:46,952
Quand je pense limites,

245
00:11:46,994 --> 00:11:50,706
je pense à un test de Leslie Perlow, spécialiste en organisation.

246
00:11:50,748 --> 00:11:52,750
Dans une des entreprises de Fortune 500,

247
00:11:52,750 --> 00:11:55,127
elle a testé un système de périodes calmes.

248
00:11:55,169 --> 00:11:58,505
Aucune interruption trois matins par semaine avant midi.

249
00:11:59,423 --> 00:12:02,384
En moyenne, la productivité des ingénieurs a explosé.

250
00:12:02,426 --> 00:12:05,596
Quarante-sept pour cent d’entre eux étaient plus productifs.

251
00:12:05,637 --> 00:12:09,933
Et quand l’entreprise a adopté cette politique de période calme,

252
00:12:09,933 --> 00:12:12,811
65 % des employés étaient plus productifs que la moyenne.

253
00:12:13,729 --> 00:12:15,606
Il n’y a rien de spécial

254
00:12:15,606 --> 00:12:18,192
à propos des mardis, jeudis et vendredis matin.

255
00:12:18,192 --> 00:12:21,612
La leçon est que nous devons traiter ces périodes ininterrompues

256
00:12:21,612 --> 00:12:23,280
comme des trésors à protéger.

257
00:12:24,698 --> 00:12:27,993
La maîtrise et la pleine conscience vous feront accéder au flow,

258
00:12:28,035 --> 00:12:31,413
mais il y a une troisième condition pour que celui-ci soit optimal.

259
00:12:31,455 --> 00:12:35,375
L’importance. Savoir que vous faites une différence dans la vie des gens.

260
00:12:36,210 --> 00:12:38,670
J’ai fait un jour une étude sur les téléprospecteurs

261
00:12:38,670 --> 00:12:42,132
qui devaient persuader des diplômés de faire un don pour une université.

262
00:12:42,132 --> 00:12:46,303
J’ai compris qu’ils se languissaient lorsque que j’ai vu ce panneau sur le mur.

263
00:12:46,345 --> 00:12:49,807
[Faire du bon boulot ici, c’est comme faire pipi dans un costume foncé.]

264
00:12:49,848 --> 00:12:52,976
[On a une sensation de chaleur, mais personne ne s’en aperçoit.]

265
00:12:52,976 --> 00:12:54,311
(Rires)

266
00:12:54,478 --> 00:12:57,731
Je voulais essayer de leur montrer que leur travail importait.

267
00:12:57,731 --> 00:13:00,108
J’ai conçu une série d’expériences.

268
00:13:00,108 --> 00:13:03,153
Durant le mois suivant, un groupe de téléprospecteurs

269
00:13:03,153 --> 00:13:05,614
a doublé son temps hebdomadaire passé au téléphone

270
00:13:05,614 --> 00:13:08,826
et presque triplé les revenus hebdomadaires.

271
00:13:08,826 --> 00:13:11,870
Ce qui a changé la donne, c’est que chacun a rencontré au hasard

272
00:13:11,912 --> 00:13:15,749
un étudiant dont la bourse avait été financée grâce à leur travail.

273
00:13:16,124 --> 00:13:19,628
Au lieu de se concentrer sur le procédé monotone d’un appel,

274
00:13:19,670 --> 00:13:21,797
ils étaient absorbés par un véritable but,

275
00:13:21,797 --> 00:13:23,924
trouver des fonds pour financer une bourse.

276
00:13:24,925 --> 00:13:28,929
Pensez à qui serait bien moins favorisé si votre travail n’existait pas.

277
00:13:28,971 --> 00:13:31,557
C’est à ces personnes que votre travail importe.

278
00:13:31,598 --> 00:13:34,935
Vous avez besoin de connaître leur nom, leur visage, et leur histoire.

279
00:13:34,935 --> 00:13:37,980
Vous trouverez le flow dans des projets dont ils bénéficieront.

280
00:13:39,106 --> 00:13:42,401
C’est pour ça que Mario Kart a été une expérience géniale pour moi.

281
00:13:42,693 --> 00:13:44,736
J’avais l’impression de maîtriser le jeu.

282
00:13:44,736 --> 00:13:48,532
Quel bonheur que de placer parfaitement une peau de banane

283
00:13:48,532 --> 00:13:50,576
pour que ma sœur glisse dessus.

284
00:13:51,743 --> 00:13:53,704
Il fallait aussi de la pleine conscience.

285
00:13:53,704 --> 00:13:55,455
Mon beau-frère était le meilleur.

286
00:13:55,455 --> 00:13:57,791
Pour le battre, il fallait être très concentré,

287
00:13:57,833 --> 00:14:00,711
surtout quand mes enfants et lui se liguaient contre moi.

288
00:14:01,837 --> 00:14:04,590
Ce n’était pas juste un jeu. Ça comptait.

289
00:14:05,465 --> 00:14:07,968
L’année dernière, on s’est tous sentis impuissants,

290
00:14:08,010 --> 00:14:09,720
d’une façon ou d’une autre.

291
00:14:09,761 --> 00:14:11,597
Je me sentais désarmé face à la Covid.

292
00:14:11,597 --> 00:14:14,516
Je n’arrivais même pas à améliorer vraiment l’école en ligne.

293
00:14:14,558 --> 00:14:15,767
Et je suis enseignant.

294
00:14:16,476 --> 00:14:19,855
Avec Mario Kart, j’avais l’impression d’aider.

295
00:14:19,855 --> 00:14:22,232
Je pouvais donner à mes enfants de quoi se réjouir

296
00:14:22,232 --> 00:14:24,067
quand on ne pouvait aller nulle part.

297
00:14:24,109 --> 00:14:27,237
On passait du temps ensemble, malgré l’éloignement.

298
00:14:28,530 --> 00:14:31,116
On pense que le flow est une expérience individuelle.

299
00:14:31,116 --> 00:14:34,411
Mais quand on jouait à la Nintendo, c’était une immersion collective.

300
00:14:34,453 --> 00:14:36,413
Bien qu’on ne joue plus tous les jours,

301
00:14:36,413 --> 00:14:38,999
je me sens plus proche de ma sœur et de mon beau-frère

302
00:14:39,041 --> 00:14:41,043
que je ne l’ai jamais été.

303
00:14:41,084 --> 00:14:44,755
J’ai appris que l’amour ne repose pas sur la fréquence des échanges,

304
00:14:44,796 --> 00:14:46,423
mais sur l’intensité de ceux-ci.

305
00:14:47,674 --> 00:14:50,552
J’ai aussi compris que l’antidote à la langueur

306
00:14:50,594 --> 00:14:53,013
ne devait pas nécessairement être productif,

307
00:14:53,055 --> 00:14:54,932
il pouvait aussi être jouissif.

308
00:14:55,766 --> 00:15:00,604
Nos meilleurs moments de flow, c’est s’amuser avec les gens qu’on aime.

309
00:15:00,604 --> 00:15:04,024
Et cela fait partie de ma liste journalière des choses à faire.

310
00:15:04,983 --> 00:15:06,985
Quelle est votre version de Mario Kart ?

311
00:15:06,985 --> 00:15:09,529
Où trouvez-vous la maîtrise et la pleine conscience

312
00:15:09,529 --> 00:15:11,657
avec les personnes qui comptent pour vous ?

313
00:15:11,782 --> 00:15:14,034
Je pense que l’on doit revoir notre conception

314
00:15:14,034 --> 00:15:15,827
de la santé mentale et du bien-être.

315
00:15:15,869 --> 00:15:20,040
Ne pas être déprimé ne veut pas dire qu’on ne peine pas.

316
00:15:20,082 --> 00:15:21,667
Ne pas être épuisé

317
00:15:21,708 --> 00:15:23,877
ne veut pas dire qu’on est motivé.

318
00:15:23,919 --> 00:15:26,254
Quand quelqu’un demande, « Ça va ? »,

319
00:15:26,296 --> 00:15:29,883
C’est OK de répondre, « Franchement, je me languis. »

320
00:15:30,884 --> 00:15:33,720
Ou si vous ne pouvez prononcer qu’une syllabe,

321
00:15:33,762 --> 00:15:35,013
« Bof. »

322
00:15:35,055 --> 00:15:36,306
(Rires)

323
00:15:36,348 --> 00:15:39,309
Et quand vous serez prêt, commencez à trouver le flow

324
00:15:39,309 --> 00:15:41,812
qui illumine le chemin hors du vide.

325
00:15:41,812 --> 00:15:42,854
Merci.

326
00:15:42,896 --> 00:15:46,191
(Applaudissements)
en_source:
1
00:00:00,413 --> 00:00:02,707
I know you all have long to-do lists,

2
00:00:02,749 --> 00:00:07,503
but I hate wasting time so much that I have a to-don't list.

3
00:00:07,545 --> 00:00:09,547
Don't scroll on social media,

4
00:00:09,589 --> 00:00:11,257
don't check my phone in bed

5
00:00:11,299 --> 00:00:12,800
and don't turn on the TV

6
00:00:12,842 --> 00:00:15,678
unless I already know what I want to watch.

7
00:00:15,720 --> 00:00:19,057
But last year I found myself breaking all of those rules.

8
00:00:19,098 --> 00:00:21,059
I was staying up way past midnight,

9
00:00:21,059 --> 00:00:22,351
doomscrolling,

10
00:00:22,351 --> 00:00:24,979
playing endless games of online Scrabble

11
00:00:24,979 --> 00:00:29,776
and bingeing entire seasons of TV shows that weren't even good.

12
00:00:30,777 --> 00:00:32,653
The next morning I'd wake up in a daze

13
00:00:32,653 --> 00:00:35,573
and swear, "Tonight in bed by 10:00."

14
00:00:35,573 --> 00:00:39,118
But it kept happening night after night for weeks.

15
00:00:39,160 --> 00:00:40,411
What was I thinking?

16
00:00:41,204 --> 00:00:42,830
As an organizational psychologist,

17
00:00:42,872 --> 00:00:45,625
I have spent my whole career studying motivation,

18
00:00:45,666 --> 00:00:50,463
so it really bothers me when I can't explain my own behavior.

19
00:00:50,505 --> 00:00:55,593
I wasn't depressed. I still had hope. Wasn't burned out, had energy.

20
00:00:55,635 --> 00:00:58,429
Wasn't lonely, I was with my family.

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:03,017
I just felt a little bit aimless and a little bit joyless.

22
00:01:03,017 --> 00:01:06,020
Eventually, I remembered there's a name for that feeling:

23
00:01:06,020 --> 00:01:07,396
languishing.

24
00:01:07,396 --> 00:01:11,234
Languishing as a sense of emptiness, stagnation and ennui.

25
00:01:11,234 --> 00:01:13,778
It was coined by a sociologist Corey Keyes

26
00:01:13,778 --> 00:01:16,989
and immortalized by a philosopher, Mariah Carey.

27
00:01:16,989 --> 00:01:19,033
(Laughter)

28
00:01:19,033 --> 00:01:22,745
When you're languishing, it just feels like you're muddling through your days,

29
00:01:22,787 --> 00:01:25,164
looking at your life through a foggy windshield.

30
00:01:25,206 --> 00:01:29,168
So I'm curious how many of you have felt like that over the past few months.

31
00:01:31,379 --> 00:01:34,507
OK, those of you who didn't have the energy to raise your hands --

32
00:01:34,549 --> 00:01:35,675
(Laughter)

33
00:01:35,716 --> 00:01:38,469
you might be languishing right now.

34
00:01:38,511 --> 00:01:41,722
And you over here who didn't laugh, you're definitely languishing.

35
00:01:43,099 --> 00:01:44,350
Strangely enough --

36
00:01:44,350 --> 00:01:46,561
[How are you feeling today? Meh. Meh. Meh.]

37
00:01:46,602 --> 00:01:47,603
(Laughter)

38
00:01:47,603 --> 00:01:49,021
Some of you passed the quiz.

39
00:01:49,939 --> 00:01:55,319
Strangely enough, what rescued me from that feeling was playing Mario Kart.

40
00:01:56,028 --> 00:01:57,530
But let's back up for a second.

41
00:01:58,322 --> 00:01:59,740
In the early days of covid,

42
00:01:59,740 --> 00:02:03,911
a lot of us were struggling with fear, grief and isolation.

43
00:02:03,953 --> 00:02:06,873
But as the pandemic dragged on with no end in sight,

44
00:02:06,914 --> 00:02:10,209
our acute anguish gave way to chronic languish.

45
00:02:10,251 --> 00:02:12,170
We were all living in “Groundhog Day.”

46
00:02:12,211 --> 00:02:14,964
It felt like the whole world was stagnating.

47
00:02:15,006 --> 00:02:18,509
So I wrote an article to put languishing on the map.

48
00:02:18,551 --> 00:02:22,054
I called it "the neglected middle child of mental health"

49
00:02:22,096 --> 00:02:25,892
and I suggested it might be the dominant emotion of our time.

50
00:02:25,933 --> 00:02:27,852
And soon it was everywhere.

51
00:02:27,852 --> 00:02:29,604
I was seeing it all over the media,

52
00:02:29,604 --> 00:02:32,356
being discussed by celebrities, by royalty.

53
00:02:32,356 --> 00:02:34,650
I've never seen people so excited

54
00:02:34,650 --> 00:02:37,278
to talk about their utter lack of excitement.

55
00:02:37,278 --> 00:02:40,198
(Laughter)

56
00:02:40,198 --> 00:02:41,449
And --

57
00:02:42,366 --> 00:02:43,910
I think --

58
00:02:43,951 --> 00:02:45,870
I think that naming languishing

59
00:02:45,912 --> 00:02:49,207
helped people make sense of some puzzling experiences.

60
00:02:49,248 --> 00:02:51,042
Why even after getting vaccinated

61
00:02:51,083 --> 00:02:54,462
people were having trouble looking forward to the rest of the year.

62
00:02:54,503 --> 00:02:57,173
Why when "National Treasure" came on TV,

63
00:02:57,215 --> 00:03:00,468
my wife already knew all the words by heart.

64
00:03:00,509 --> 00:03:03,471
And why I was staying up way too late,

65
00:03:03,512 --> 00:03:07,475
falling victim to what's known as revenge bedtime procrastination.

66
00:03:07,516 --> 00:03:08,768
(Laughter)

67
00:03:17,693 --> 00:03:22,949
We were looking for bliss in a blah day and purpose in a perpetual pandemic.

68
00:03:23,908 --> 00:03:26,619
But languishing is not unique to a pandemic.

69
00:03:26,661 --> 00:03:28,704
It's part of the human condition.

70
00:03:28,746 --> 00:03:32,166
Two decades of research show that languishing can disrupt your focus

71
00:03:32,208 --> 00:03:34,252
and dampen your motivation.

72
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,671
It's also a risk factor for depression

73
00:03:36,712 --> 00:03:39,840
because languishing often lurks below the surface.

74
00:03:39,882 --> 00:03:42,426
You might not notice when your drive is dwindling

75
00:03:42,468 --> 00:03:44,595
or your delight is dulling

76
00:03:44,637 --> 00:03:47,598
You’re indifferent to your own indifference,

77
00:03:47,640 --> 00:03:49,433
which means you don't seek help

78
00:03:49,433 --> 00:03:53,646
and you might not even do anything to help yourself.

79
00:03:53,646 --> 00:03:54,897
Meh.

80
00:03:56,065 --> 00:03:59,235
Languishing isn't just hard to spot, though.

81
00:03:59,235 --> 00:04:02,571
In many cultures, it's hard to talk about, too.

82
00:04:02,571 --> 00:04:04,448
When people ask, "How are you?,"

83
00:04:04,448 --> 00:04:06,659
you're expected to say, "Great!"

84
00:04:06,659 --> 00:04:09,036
or "Living my best life."

85
00:04:09,954 --> 00:04:12,123
That's called toxic positivity.

86
00:04:12,164 --> 00:04:14,292
(Laughter)

87
00:04:16,919 --> 00:04:23,217
It's the pressure that we face to be optimistic and upbeat at all times.

88
00:04:23,259 --> 00:04:25,803
If you say, "You know, I'm just OK,"

89
00:04:25,845 --> 00:04:29,098
then people might encourage you to look on the bright side

90
00:04:29,140 --> 00:04:31,475
or count your blessings,

91
00:04:31,475 --> 00:04:32,935
which isn't just annoying.

92
00:04:32,935 --> 00:04:35,021
It can actually be bad advice.

93
00:04:35,021 --> 00:04:36,772
Can I get two volunteers?

94
00:04:37,690 --> 00:04:40,276
I will cold-call if I have to, don't all jump at once.

95
00:04:40,276 --> 00:04:41,902
OK, right over here.

96
00:04:41,902 --> 00:04:43,195
You can come up to a mic

97
00:04:43,195 --> 00:04:46,991
and can I get another volunteer right over there, up to this mic, please.

98
00:04:46,991 --> 00:04:49,035
A round of applause for our two volunteers.

99
00:04:49,076 --> 00:04:50,870
(Applause)

100
00:04:52,872 --> 00:04:54,373
Hi, what's your name?

101
00:04:54,415 --> 00:04:55,458
Martin: Martin.

102
00:04:55,458 --> 00:04:56,584
Adam Grant: Thank you.

103
00:04:56,584 --> 00:04:59,628
Can you tell us three good things about your life, please?

104
00:05:01,672 --> 00:05:06,177
Martin: I’m married and I’m healthy and I’m happy.

105
00:05:06,218 --> 00:05:08,679
AG: All right, I’m glad the marriage came in first.

106
00:05:08,679 --> 00:05:10,848
Well done. OK, over here. What’s your name?

107
00:05:10,890 --> 00:05:11,891
Lee: Lee.

108
00:05:11,891 --> 00:05:14,727
AG: Lee, can you tell us 42 good things about your life?

109
00:05:16,270 --> 00:05:19,398
Lee: My cat Titchypoo, my dog Enzo.

110
00:05:19,398 --> 00:05:20,775
And so my wife, Jazz.

111
00:05:20,775 --> 00:05:22,568
AG: Third behind the dog and the cat.

112
00:05:22,568 --> 00:05:23,569
(Laughter)

113
00:05:23,611 --> 00:05:25,071
Well played.

114
00:05:25,071 --> 00:05:27,156
Lee: My children, Indio and Walter,

115
00:05:27,156 --> 00:05:28,866
Manchester United Football Club,

116
00:05:28,866 --> 00:05:30,659
my friends, TED.

117
00:05:31,702 --> 00:05:33,496
AG: TED coming in at ringing eighth.

118
00:05:33,496 --> 00:05:35,581
Lee: TED is very high, TED is very high.

119
00:05:35,623 --> 00:05:40,753
The poetry of C.S. Lewis, E.E. Cummings, Dylan Thomas.

120
00:05:40,795 --> 00:05:43,506
AG: You want to name all the poets you’ve ever heard of?

121
00:05:43,506 --> 00:05:45,341
Alright, Lee, thank you.

122
00:05:45,341 --> 00:05:46,842
We’re going to pause you there.

123
00:05:46,884 --> 00:05:48,886
Round of applause. Thank you both.

124
00:05:48,928 --> 00:05:50,638
(Applause)

125
00:05:52,473 --> 00:05:56,435
So for a long time, I assumed that people in Lee's position

126
00:05:56,435 --> 00:05:59,105
were going to be happier than Martin.

127
00:05:59,105 --> 00:06:02,483
But when I ran the experiment, I found the exact opposite.

128
00:06:02,483 --> 00:06:05,736
That people who are randomly assigned to count more blessings,

129
00:06:05,736 --> 00:06:08,280
are actually, on average, less happy

130
00:06:08,280 --> 00:06:11,242
because you start to run out of things to be optimistic about.

131
00:06:11,242 --> 00:06:13,244
And if you don't know that many poets ...

132
00:06:13,285 --> 00:06:14,995
(Laughter)

133
00:06:15,037 --> 00:06:18,249
The harder it is to find good things about your life,

134
00:06:18,290 --> 00:06:23,087
the more you feel like, well, maybe my life isn't that good.

135
00:06:23,129 --> 00:06:24,755
In the early days of the pandemic,

136
00:06:24,797 --> 00:06:29,635
researchers found that the best predictor of well-being was not optimism.

137
00:06:29,677 --> 00:06:30,928
It was flow.

138
00:06:31,971 --> 00:06:34,640
Flow is that feeling of being in the zone,

139
00:06:34,682 --> 00:06:37,268
coined by the psychologist Mihály Csíkszentmihályi.

140
00:06:38,144 --> 00:06:40,813
It's that state of total absorption in an activity.

141
00:06:40,813 --> 00:06:45,484
For you, it might be cooking or running or gardening

142
00:06:45,484 --> 00:06:46,986
where you lose track of time

143
00:06:46,986 --> 00:06:50,364
and you might even lose your sense of self.

144
00:06:50,364 --> 00:06:52,992
Flow is the appeal of a Netflix binge

145
00:06:52,992 --> 00:06:55,327
because you get transported into a different world

146
00:06:55,369 --> 00:06:57,163
and immersed in a story.

147
00:06:57,204 --> 00:07:02,084
But bingeing is a temporary escape from languishing, not a cure.

148
00:07:02,126 --> 00:07:05,921
At best, it leaves you with a bunch of asymmetric relationships.

149
00:07:05,963 --> 00:07:08,549
You might love hanging out with your friends:

150
00:07:08,591 --> 00:07:10,342
Chandler,

151
00:07:10,384 --> 00:07:11,635
Arya,

152
00:07:11,677 --> 00:07:12,970
Dwight,

153
00:07:13,012 --> 00:07:14,388
Buffy -- Buffy, anyone? --

154
00:07:14,430 --> 00:07:15,473
(Laughter)

155
00:07:15,514 --> 00:07:16,765
Joe Exotic,

156
00:07:17,766 --> 00:07:18,976
Peppa Pig.

157
00:07:19,643 --> 00:07:21,896
(Whispers) But they don't know you exist.

158
00:07:23,814 --> 00:07:27,693
Bingeing is passive engagement in a fictional world,

159
00:07:27,693 --> 00:07:30,571
peak flow depends on active participation

160
00:07:30,571 --> 00:07:32,323
in the real world,

161
00:07:32,323 --> 00:07:35,659
which is why I was so surprised to find my flow

162
00:07:35,701 --> 00:07:39,079
while driving a cartoon car in a Nintendo game.

163
00:07:41,040 --> 00:07:42,583
When the pandemic first started,

164
00:07:42,625 --> 00:07:45,711
all three of our kids were at home in online school,

165
00:07:45,753 --> 00:07:47,922
and that lasted for a full year.

166
00:07:47,963 --> 00:07:49,882
It was not easy.

167
00:07:49,924 --> 00:07:52,593
One day we found this on our six-year-old's report card.

168
00:07:52,676 --> 00:07:57,515
[can independently mute and unmute himself when requested to do so]

169
00:07:57,556 --> 00:07:59,183
(Laughter)

170
00:08:00,017 --> 00:08:03,979
You know, I know some adults who still haven't figured that out yet,

171
00:08:03,979 --> 00:08:06,815
not just online, but in real life, too.

172
00:08:06,815 --> 00:08:09,527
So I guess we had that to celebrate.

173
00:08:09,527 --> 00:08:13,781
But like many of you, we were isolated from extended family.

174
00:08:13,781 --> 00:08:16,367
My sister was halfway across the country.

175
00:08:16,367 --> 00:08:19,995
And one day we were reminiscing about how much we love playing Mario Kart

176
00:08:20,037 --> 00:08:21,330
as we were kids.

177
00:08:21,372 --> 00:08:24,458
And she said, "Well, we could all play together online now."

178
00:08:24,500 --> 00:08:26,752
Why don't we start a family game?

179
00:08:26,794 --> 00:08:28,587
And soon we were playing every day

180
00:08:28,629 --> 00:08:30,756
with a video call running at the same time.

181
00:08:31,632 --> 00:08:35,469
And after a couple of weeks I stopped feeling so blah.

182
00:08:35,511 --> 00:08:38,222
I was living zen in the art of Mario Kart.

183
00:08:38,264 --> 00:08:39,515
(Laughter)

184
00:08:39,557 --> 00:08:43,852
In the morning our kids were waking up, asking what time we would play.

185
00:08:43,852 --> 00:08:45,479
They were excited.

186
00:08:45,479 --> 00:08:48,774
And they loved it when I would gloat about an impending victory,

187
00:08:48,774 --> 00:08:51,527
only to be bombed by a flying blue shell

188
00:08:51,527 --> 00:08:54,154
and then just sit there watching all three of our kids

189
00:08:54,154 --> 00:08:57,074
drive past me to the finish line in tiny go-carts.

190
00:08:58,075 --> 00:09:01,412
We had so much fun that we started a new Saturday night tradition

191
00:09:01,453 --> 00:09:03,080
after the kids were asleep.

192
00:09:03,122 --> 00:09:04,415
Adult Mario Kart.

193
00:09:04,456 --> 00:09:05,666
(Laughter)

194
00:09:07,918 --> 00:09:09,962
So after reflecting on that experience,

195
00:09:10,004 --> 00:09:13,090
I'm proud to present to you for the first time

196
00:09:13,132 --> 00:09:15,926
my Mario Kart theory of peak flow.

197
00:09:17,094 --> 00:09:21,974
It has three conditions: mastery, mindfulness and mattering.

198
00:09:22,016 --> 00:09:23,726
Let's start with mastery.

199
00:09:23,726 --> 00:09:25,019
Mastery is something

200
00:09:25,019 --> 00:09:27,730
a lot of us have been having a hard time finding lately.

201
00:09:27,730 --> 00:09:28,897
(Laughter)

202
00:09:28,897 --> 00:09:31,191
Psychologists find that at work

203
00:09:31,191 --> 00:09:34,278
the strongest factor in daily motivation and joy

204
00:09:34,278 --> 00:09:36,030
is a sense of progress.

205
00:09:36,947 --> 00:09:40,492
We find that our happiness depends in Western cultures

206
00:09:40,492 --> 00:09:44,747
more on how our projects are going today than how they went yesterday.

207
00:09:44,788 --> 00:09:46,874
That's why Nike says, "Just do it."

208
00:09:47,958 --> 00:09:52,212
I guess if Nike had been started in a more past-focused country like China,

209
00:09:52,254 --> 00:09:54,506
their slogan would be, "Just did it."

210
00:09:57,176 --> 00:10:01,722
If languishing is stagnation, flow involves momentum.

211
00:10:01,764 --> 00:10:05,017
But mastery does not have to be a big accomplishment,

212
00:10:05,059 --> 00:10:07,102
it can be small wins.

213
00:10:07,102 --> 00:10:10,356
Small wins explain why I was drawn to online Scrabble

214
00:10:10,356 --> 00:10:13,525
for the rush of playing a seven-letter word.

215
00:10:13,525 --> 00:10:16,362
Small wins makes sense of why so many people were thrilled

216
00:10:16,362 --> 00:10:18,947
to bake their first loaf of sourdough bread.

217
00:10:18,947 --> 00:10:23,577
And small wins explain why one engineer spent an entire afternoon

218
00:10:23,619 --> 00:10:28,374
mastering the art of stacking M&M's on top of each other.

219
00:10:28,415 --> 00:10:29,667
Take a look.

220
00:10:30,334 --> 00:10:32,711
(Video) This is going to be harder than I thought.

221
00:10:39,468 --> 00:10:40,761
Oh!

222
00:10:41,261 --> 00:10:42,721
Oh!

223
00:10:42,763 --> 00:10:44,598
Five M&Ms! Five M&Ms!

224
00:10:46,141 --> 00:10:47,351
(Laughter)

225
00:10:48,560 --> 00:10:50,562
AG: Turns out that was a world record.

226
00:10:50,562 --> 00:10:52,147
(Laughter)

227
00:10:52,147 --> 00:10:55,818
That kind of mastery depends on a second condition for flow,

228
00:10:55,818 --> 00:10:57,111
mindfulness.

229
00:10:57,111 --> 00:11:00,447
Focusing your full attention on a single task,

230
00:11:00,447 --> 00:11:04,201
not something a lot of us are doing that much these days.

231
00:11:04,243 --> 00:11:07,913
[Are you OK? You’re barely paying attention to your book, phone, show ... ]

232
00:11:07,913 --> 00:11:10,791
[ ... laptop and the crossword you started ten minutes ago.]

233
00:11:10,833 --> 00:11:15,879
There's evidence that on average, people are checking emails 74 times a day,

234
00:11:15,921 --> 00:11:20,008
switching tasks every 10 minutes,

235
00:11:20,050 --> 00:11:23,011
and that creates what's been called time confetti,

236
00:11:23,053 --> 00:11:25,848
where we take what could be meaningful moments of our lives

237
00:11:25,889 --> 00:11:27,141
and we shred them

238
00:11:27,182 --> 00:11:29,560
into increasingly tiny, useless pieces.

239
00:11:30,769 --> 00:11:34,982
Time confetti is an enemy of both energy and of excellence.

240
00:11:34,982 --> 00:11:37,985
If we want to find flow, we need better boundaries.

241
00:11:38,068 --> 00:11:42,448
[It keeps me from looking at my phone every two seconds.]

242
00:11:42,614 --> 00:11:43,907
(Laughter)

243
00:11:45,492 --> 00:11:46,952
When I think about boundaries,

244
00:11:46,994 --> 00:11:50,706
I think of an experiment by organizational scholar Leslie Perlow.

245
00:11:50,748 --> 00:11:55,127
She went to a Fortune 500 company and she tested a quiet time policy.

246
00:11:55,169 --> 00:11:58,505
No interruptions three mornings a week before noon.

247
00:11:59,423 --> 00:12:02,384
On average, engineers spiked in productivity.

248
00:12:02,426 --> 00:12:05,596
47 percent of them were more productive than usual.

249
00:12:05,637 --> 00:12:09,933
But the best part is that when the company made quiet time official policy,

250
00:12:09,933 --> 00:12:12,811
they had 65 percent above average productivity.

251
00:12:13,729 --> 00:12:15,606
I don't think there's anything magical

252
00:12:15,606 --> 00:12:18,192
about Tuesday, Thursday, Friday before noon.

253
00:12:18,192 --> 00:12:21,612
The lesson here is that we need to treat uninterrupted blocks of time

254
00:12:21,612 --> 00:12:23,280
as treasures to guard.

255
00:12:24,698 --> 00:12:27,993
Now, mastery and mindfulness will get you to flow,

256
00:12:28,035 --> 00:12:31,413
but there's a third condition that turns it into a peak experience.

257
00:12:31,455 --> 00:12:35,375
Mattering. Knowing that you make a difference to other people.

258
00:12:36,293 --> 00:12:38,879
Early in my career, I was studying fundraising callers

259
00:12:38,921 --> 00:12:42,090
who were trying to bring in alumni donations to a university,

260
00:12:42,132 --> 00:12:46,303
and I knew they were languishing when I saw this sign posted on their wall.

261
00:12:46,345 --> 00:12:50,349
[Doing a good job here is like wetting your pants in a dark suit]

262
00:12:50,390 --> 00:12:52,684
[You get a warm feeling but no one else notices]

263
00:12:52,726 --> 00:12:54,311
(Laughter)

264
00:12:54,478 --> 00:12:57,731
I wanted to study how to show them that their work mattered.

265
00:12:57,731 --> 00:13:00,108
So I designed a series of experiments

266
00:13:00,108 --> 00:13:03,153
and over the next month, one group of callers on average

267
00:13:03,153 --> 00:13:05,614
more than doubled in weekly time on the phone

268
00:13:05,614 --> 00:13:08,826
and nearly tripled in weekly revenue.

269
00:13:08,826 --> 00:13:12,788
What moved the needle was randomly assigning them to meet one student

270
00:13:12,830 --> 00:13:15,207
whose scholarship had been funded by their work.

271
00:13:16,124 --> 00:13:19,628
Now, instead of focusing on the monotonous process of making calls,

272
00:13:19,670 --> 00:13:23,924
they were absorbed in a meaningful purpose of helping to fund tuition.

273
00:13:24,925 --> 00:13:28,929
So think about the people who would be worse off if your job didn't exist.

274
00:13:28,971 --> 00:13:31,557
Those are the people who make your work matter.

275
00:13:31,598 --> 00:13:35,060
You need to know their names, their faces and their stories,

276
00:13:35,060 --> 00:13:37,813
and you can find flow in projects that benefit them.

277
00:13:39,106 --> 00:13:42,818
This all explains why Mario Kart was such a great experience for me.

278
00:13:42,818 --> 00:13:44,736
It gave me a feeling of mastery,

279
00:13:44,736 --> 00:13:48,532
the sweet satisfaction of a perfectly placed banana peel

280
00:13:48,532 --> 00:13:50,576
for my sister to slip on.

281
00:13:51,910 --> 00:13:53,620
It required mindfulness too.

282
00:13:53,662 --> 00:13:55,539
My brother-in-law was the best player.

283
00:13:55,581 --> 00:13:57,791
Beating him demanded total concentration,

284
00:13:57,833 --> 00:14:00,711
especially when my kids were ganging up with him against me.

285
00:14:01,837 --> 00:14:04,590
And it wasn't just a game. It mattered.

286
00:14:05,465 --> 00:14:09,720
Over the past year, we've all felt helpless in one way or another.

287
00:14:09,761 --> 00:14:11,638
I felt helpless to fix covid.

288
00:14:11,680 --> 00:14:14,516
I couldn't even do that much to make online school better.

289
00:14:14,558 --> 00:14:15,767
And I'm a teacher.

290
00:14:16,476 --> 00:14:19,855
But in Mario Kart, I felt helpful.

291
00:14:19,855 --> 00:14:22,524
I was able to give my kids something to look forward to

292
00:14:22,524 --> 00:14:24,026
when we couldn't go anywhere.

293
00:14:24,026 --> 00:14:27,237
I was able to keep my family close when we were far apart.

294
00:14:28,530 --> 00:14:31,116
We normally think of flow as an individual experience.

295
00:14:31,116 --> 00:14:34,411
But playing Nintendo, we were all immersed together.

296
00:14:34,453 --> 00:14:36,496
And although we don't play daily anymore,

297
00:14:36,538 --> 00:14:38,999
I feel closer to my sister and my brother-in-law

298
00:14:39,041 --> 00:14:41,043
than I ever had before.

299
00:14:41,084 --> 00:14:44,755
I learned that love is not the frequency of communication,

300
00:14:44,796 --> 00:14:46,298
it's the depth of connections.

301
00:14:47,674 --> 00:14:50,552
I also realized that the antidote to languishing

302
00:14:50,594 --> 00:14:53,013
does not have to be something productive,

303
00:14:53,055 --> 00:14:54,932
it can be something joyful.

304
00:14:55,766 --> 00:15:00,604
Our peak moments of flow are having fun with the people we love,

305
00:15:00,604 --> 00:15:04,024
which is now a daily task on my to-do list.

306
00:15:04,983 --> 00:15:06,985
So what's your version of Mario Kart?

307
00:15:06,985 --> 00:15:10,697
Where do you find mastery and mindfulness with the people who matter to you?

308
00:15:11,782 --> 00:15:13,951
I think we need to rethink our understanding

309
00:15:13,951 --> 00:15:15,827
of mental health and well-being.

310
00:15:15,869 --> 00:15:20,040
Not depressed doesn't mean you're not struggling.

311
00:15:20,082 --> 00:15:21,667
Not burned out

312
00:15:21,708 --> 00:15:23,877
doesn't mean you're fired up.

313
00:15:23,919 --> 00:15:26,254
When someone says, "How are you?,"

314
00:15:26,296 --> 00:15:29,883
it's OK to say, "Honestly, I'm languishing."

315
00:15:30,884 --> 00:15:33,720
Or if you can only muster one syllable,

316
00:15:33,762 --> 00:15:35,013
"Meh."

317
00:15:35,055 --> 00:15:36,306
(Laughter)

318
00:15:36,348 --> 00:15:39,309
And when you're ready, you can start finding the flow

319
00:15:39,309 --> 00:15:41,812
that lights a path out of the void.

320
00:15:41,812 --> 00:15:42,854
Thank you.

321
00:15:42,896 --> 00:15:46,191
(Applause)

Full English transcript to translate (translate ALL cues, do not stop until the last one):

1
00:00:00,413 --> 00:00:02,707
I know you all have long to-do lists,

2
00:00:02,749 --> 00:00:07,503
but I hate wasting time so much that I have a to-don't list.

3
00:00:07,545 --> 00:00:09,547
Don't scroll on social media,

4
00:00:09,589 --> 00:00:11,257
don't check my phone in bed

5
00:00:11,299 --> 00:00:12,800
and don't turn on the TV

6
00:00:12,842 --> 00:00:15,678
unless I already know what I want to watch.

7
00:00:15,720 --> 00:00:19,057
But last year I found myself breaking all of those rules.

8
00:00:19,098 --> 00:00:21,059
I was staying up way past midnight,

9
00:00:21,059 --> 00:00:22,351
doomscrolling,

10
00:00:22,351 --> 00:00:24,979
playing endless games of online Scrabble

11
00:00:24,979 --> 00:00:29,776
and bingeing entire seasons of TV shows that weren't even good.

12
00:00:30,777 --> 00:00:32,653
The next morning I'd wake up in a daze

13
00:00:32,653 --> 00:00:35,573
and swear, "Tonight in bed by 10:00."

14
00:00:35,573 --> 00:00:39,118
But it kept happening night after night for weeks.

15
00:00:39,160 --> 00:00:40,411
What was I thinking?

16
00:00:41,204 --> 00:00:42,830
As an organizational psychologist,

17
00:00:42,872 --> 00:00:45,625
I have spent my whole career studying motivation,

18
00:00:45,666 --> 00:00:50,463
so it really bothers me when I can't explain my own behavior.

19
00:00:50,505 --> 00:00:55,593
I wasn't depressed. I still had hope. Wasn't burned out, had energy.

20
00:00:55,635 --> 00:00:58,429
Wasn't lonely, I was with my family.

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:03,017
I just felt a little bit aimless and a little bit joyless.

22
00:01:03,017 --> 00:01:06,020
Eventually, I remembered there's a name for that feeling:

23
00:01:06,020 --> 00:01:07,396
languishing.

24
00:01:07,396 --> 00:01:11,234
Languishing as a sense of emptiness, stagnation and ennui.

25
00:01:11,234 --> 00:01:13,778
It was coined by a sociologist Corey Keyes

26
00:01:13,778 --> 00:01:16,989
and immortalized by a philosopher, Mariah Carey.

27
00:01:16,989 --> 00:01:19,033
(Laughter)

28
00:01:19,033 --> 00:01:22,745
When you're languishing, it just feels like you're muddling through your days,

29
00:01:22,787 --> 00:01:25,164
looking at your life through a foggy windshield.

30
00:01:25,206 --> 00:01:29,168
So I'm curious how many of you have felt like that over the past few months.

31
00:01:31,379 --> 00:01:34,507
OK, those of you who didn't have the energy to raise your hands --

32
00:01:34,549 --> 00:01:35,675
(Laughter)

33
00:01:35,716 --> 00:01:38,469
you might be languishing right now.

34
00:01:38,511 --> 00:01:41,722
And you over here who didn't laugh, you're definitely languishing.

35
00:01:43,099 --> 00:01:44,350
Strangely enough --

36
00:01:44,350 --> 00:01:46,561
[How are you feeling today? Meh. Meh. Meh.]

37
00:01:46,602 --> 00:01:47,603
(Laughter)

38
00:01:47,603 --> 00:01:49,021
Some of you passed the quiz.

39
00:01:49,939 --> 00:01:55,319
Strangely enough, what rescued me from that feeling was playing Mario Kart.

40
00:01:56,028 --> 00:01:57,530
But let's back up for a second.

41
00:01:58,322 --> 00:01:59,740
In the early days of covid,

42
00:01:59,740 --> 00:02:03,911
a lot of us were struggling with fear, grief and isolation.

43
00:02:03,953 --> 00:02:06,873
But as the pandemic dragged on with no end in sight,

44
00:02:06,914 --> 00:02:10,209
our acute anguish gave way to chronic languish.

45
00:02:10,251 --> 00:02:12,170
We were all living in “Groundhog Day.”

46
00:02:12,211 --> 00:02:14,964
It felt like the whole world was stagnating.

47
00:02:15,006 --> 00:02:18,509
So I wrote an article to put languishing on the map.

48
00:02:18,551 --> 00:02:22,054
I called it "the neglected middle child of mental health"

49
00:02:22,096 --> 00:02:25,892
and I suggested it might be the dominant emotion of our time.

50
00:02:25,933 --> 00:02:27,852
And soon it was everywhere.

51
00:02:27,852 --> 00:02:29,604
I was seeing it all over the media,

52
00:02:29,604 --> 00:02:32,356
being discussed by celebrities, by royalty.

53
00:02:32,356 --> 00:02:34,650
I've never seen people so excited

54
00:02:34,650 --> 00:02:37,278
to talk about their utter lack of excitement.

55
00:02:37,278 --> 00:02:40,198
(Laughter)

56
00:02:40,198 --> 00:02:41,449
And --

57
00:02:42,366 --> 00:02:43,910
I think --

58
00:02:43,951 --> 00:02:45,870
I think that naming languishing

59
00:02:45,912 --> 00:02:49,207
helped people make sense of some puzzling experiences.

60
00:02:49,248 --> 00:02:51,042
Why even after getting vaccinated

61
00:02:51,083 --> 00:02:54,462
people were having trouble looking forward to the rest of the year.

62
00:02:54,503 --> 00:02:57,173
Why when "National Treasure" came on TV,

63
00:02:57,215 --> 00:03:00,468
my wife already knew all the words by heart.

64
00:03:00,509 --> 00:03:03,471
And why I was staying up way too late,

65
00:03:03,512 --> 00:03:07,475
falling victim to what's known as revenge bedtime procrastination.

66
00:03:07,516 --> 00:03:08,768
(Laughter)

67
00:03:17,693 --> 00:03:22,949
We were looking for bliss in a blah day and purpose in a perpetual pandemic.

68
00:03:23,908 --> 00:03:26,619
But languishing is not unique to a pandemic.

69
00:03:26,661 --> 00:03:28,704
It's part of the human condition.

70
00:03:28,746 --> 00:03:32,166
Two decades of research show that languishing can disrupt your focus

71
00:03:32,208 --> 00:03:34,252
and dampen your motivation.

72
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,671
It's also a risk factor for depression

73
00:03:36,712 --> 00:03:39,840
because languishing often lurks below the surface.

74
00:03:39,882 --> 00:03:42,426
You might not notice when your drive is dwindling

75
00:03:42,468 --> 00:03:44,595
or your delight is dulling

76
00:03:44,637 --> 00:03:47,598
You’re indifferent to your own indifference,

77
00:03:47,640 --> 00:03:49,433
which means you don't seek help

78
00:03:49,433 --> 00:03:53,646
and you might not even do anything to help yourself.

79
00:03:53,646 --> 00:03:54,897
Meh.

80
00:03:56,065 --> 00:03:59,235
Languishing isn't just hard to spot, though.

81
00:03:59,235 --> 00:04:02,571
In many cultures, it's hard to talk about, too.

82
00:04:02,571 --> 00:04:04,448
When people ask, "How are you?,"

83
00:04:04,448 --> 00:04:06,659
you're expected to say, "Great!"

84
00:04:06,659 --> 00:04:09,036
or "Living my best life."

85
00:04:09,954 --> 00:04:12,123
That's called toxic positivity.

86
00:04:12,164 --> 00:04:14,292
(Laughter)

87
00:04:16,919 --> 00:04:23,217
It's the pressure that we face to be optimistic and upbeat at all times.

88
00:04:23,259 --> 00:04:25,803
If you say, "You know, I'm just OK,"

89
00:04:25,845 --> 00:04:29,098
then people might encourage you to look on the bright side

90
00:04:29,140 --> 00:04:31,475
or count your blessings,

91
00:04:31,475 --> 00:04:32,935
which isn't just annoying.

92
00:04:32,935 --> 00:04:35,021
It can actually be bad advice.

93
00:04:35,021 --> 00:04:36,772
Can I get two volunteers?

94
00:04:37,690 --> 00:04:40,276
I will cold-call if I have to, don't all jump at once.

95
00:04:40,276 --> 00:04:41,902
OK, right over here.

96
00:04:41,902 --> 00:04:43,195
You can come up to a mic

97
00:04:43,195 --> 00:04:46,991
and can I get another volunteer right over there, up to this mic, please.

98
00:04:46,991 --> 00:04:49,035
A round of applause for our two volunteers.

99
00:04:49,076 --> 00:04:50,870
(Applause)

100

1
00:00:00,413 --> 00:00:02,707
Je sais que vous avez tous de longues listes de choses à faire.

2
00:00:02,749 --> 00:00:07,503
Moi qui déteste perdre mon temps, j’ai une autre liste à ne pas faire.

3
00:00:07,545 --> 00:00:09,547
Ne pas parcourir les réseaux sociaux,

4
00:00:09,589 --> 00:00:11,257
ne pas regarder mon téléphone au lit,

5
00:00:11,299 --> 00:00:12,800
et ne pas allumer la télé

6
00:00:12,842 --> 00:00:15,678
sauf si je sais déjà ce que je veux voir.

7
00:00:15,720 --> 00:00:19,057
Mais il y a un an, je me suis surprise à enfreindre toutes mes règles.

8
00:00:19,098 --> 00:00:21,059
Je me couchais bien après minuit,

9
00:00:21,059 --> 00:00:22,351
je doomscrollais,

10
00:00:22,351 --> 00:00:24,979
je jouais aux parties sans fin de Scrabble en ligne

11
00:00:24,979 --> 00:00:29,776
et regardais des saisons entières de séries TV médiocres.

12
00:00:30,777 --> 00:00:32,653
Le matin, je me réveillais en état de choc

13
00:00:32,653 --> 00:00:35,573
et me jurais : « Cette fois, au lit à 22h ! »

14
00:00:35,573 --> 00:00:39,118
Mais ça se reproduisait nuit après nuit pendant des semaines.

15
00:00:39,160 --> 00:00:40,411
Qu'est-ce qui me prenait ?

16
00:00:41,204 --> 00:00:42,830
En tant que psychologue spécialisée en organisation,

17
00:00:42,872 --> 00:00:45,625
je passe ma vie à étudier la motivation –

18
00:00:45,666 --> 00:00:50,463
alors pourquoi étais-je incapable d'expliquer mon propre comportement ?

19
00:00:50,505 --> 00:00:55,593
Je n'étais pas déprimée. J'avais encore de l'espoir. Ni épuisée, j'avais de l'énergie.

20
00:00:55,635 --> 00:00:58,429
Et pas seule, j'étais avec ma famille.

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:03,017
J'avais juste l'impression de flotter, vide, comme engourdie.

22
00:01:03,017 --> 00:01:06,020
J’ai fini par me souvenir qu’il y a un nom pour ce sentiment :

23
00:01:06,020 --> 00:01:07,396
« Languissement ».

24
00:01:07,396 --> 00:01:11,234
C'est un vide, un blocage, une lassitude profonde.

25
00:01:11,234 --> 00:01:13,778
Coined par le sociologue Corey Keyes,

26
00:01:13,778 --> 00:01:16,989
popularisé par une philosophe, Mariah Carey.

27
00:01:16,989 --> 00:01:19,033
(Rires)

28
00:01:19,033 --> 00:01:22,745
Quand on languit, on se traîne comme un zombie,

29
00:01:22,787 --> 00:01:25,164
en regardant sa vie à travers un pare-brise embué.

30
00:01:25,206 --> 00:01:29,168
Combien d'entre vous se sentent comme ça depuis quelques mois ?

31
00:01:31,379 --> 00:01:34,507
D'accord, ceux qui n’ont pas eu la force de lever la main –

32
00:01:34,549 --> 00:01:35,675
(Rires)

33
00:01:35,716 --> 00:01:38,469
vous êtes probablement lassés maintenant.

34
00:01:38,511 --> 00:01:41,722
Et vous qui n’avez pas ri, vous êtes incontestablement dans cet état.

35
00:01:43,099 --> 00:01:44,350
De façon surprenante —

36
00:01:44,350 --> 00:01:46,561
Méh. Méh. Méh.

37
00:01:46,602 --> 00:01:47,603
(Rires)

38
00:01:47,603 --> 00:01:49,021
Certains d’entre vous ont passé l’épreuve.

39
00:01:49,939 --> 00:01:55,319
De façon surprenante, ce qui m’a sauvé est né sur un écran de jeu vidéo Mario Kart.

40
00:01:56,028 --> 00:01:57,530
Revenons en arrière pour comprendre.

41
00:01:58,322 --> 00:01:59,740
Au début de la pandémie,

42
00:01:59,740 --> 00:02:03,911
beaucoup luttaient contre la peur, le deuil et l’isolement.

43
00:02:03,953 --> 00:02:06,873
Mais quand la quarantaine s’est prolongée à l’infini,

44
00:02:06,914 --> 00:02:10,209
notre anxiété a cédé la place au « languissement » chronique.

45
00:02:10,251 --> 00:02:12,170
Tout le monde vivait dans Groundhog Day.

46
00:02:12,211 --> 00:02:14,964
La planète semblait être dans un état stoppé.

47
00:02:15,006 --> 00:02:18,509
J’ai donc écrit un article pour en parler.

48
00:02:18,551 --> 00:02:22,054
J’appeiais ça « l’enfant oublié de la santé mentale »

49
00:02:22,096 --> 00:02:25,892
et j’ai suggéré que c’était l’émotion prédominante de notre époque.

50
00:02:25,933 --> 00:02:27,852
Et rapidement, tout le monde en parlait.

51
00:02:27,852 --> 00:02:29,604
C’était partout dans les médias,

52
00:02:29,604 --> 00:02:32,356
abordé par des célébrités, des royautés.

53
00:02:32,356 --> 00:02:34,650
Je n’avais jamais vu autant de monde

54
00:02:34,650 --> 00:02:37,278
discuter de leur manque total d’enthousiasme.

55
00:02:37,278 --> 00:02:40,198
(Rires)

56
00:02:40,198 --> 00:02:41,449
Et —

57
00:02:42,366 --> 00:02:43,910
Je crois que

58
00:02:43,951 --> 00:02:45,870
donner un nom au « languissement »

59
00:02:45,912 --> 00:02:49,207
aide les gens à comprendre des situations étranges.

60
00:02:49,248 --> 00:02:51,042
Mais pourquoi, malgré le vaccin,

61
00:02:51,083 --> 00:02:54,462
les gens hésitaient-ils à imaginer l’avenir ?

62
00:02:54,503 --> 00:02:57,173
Pourquoi ma femme connaissait-elle par cœur tous les mots du journal télévisé ?

63
00:02:57,215 --> 00:03:00,468
Et pourquoi je me réveillais à des heures folles,

64
00:03:00,509 --> 00:03:03,471
victime de la procrastination nocturne de revanche ?

65
00:03:03,512 --> 00:03:07,475
(Rires)

66
00:03:17,693 --> 00:03:22,949
On cherchait le bonheur dans des jours fades, un sens dans le chaos de la pandémie.

67
00:03:23,908 --> 00:03:26,619
Mais le languissement n’est pas spécifique aux crises.

68
00:03:26,661 --> 00:03:28,704
C’est profondément humain.

69
00:03:28,746 --> 00:03:32,166
Vingt ans de recherches montrent que le languissement perturbe la concentration,

70
00:03:32,208 --> 00:03:34,252
assombrit la motivation,

71
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,671
et accentue le risque de dépression.

72
00:03:36,712 --> 00:03:39,840
Parce qu’il se cache souvent en amont des symptômes.

73
00:03:39,882 --> 00:03:42,426
On ne remarque pas quand on perd l’énergie,

74
00:03:42,468 --> 00:03:44,595
ni quand l’ambition s’émousse.

75
00:03:44,637 --> 00:03:47,598
On ignore sa propre apathie,

76
00:03:47,640 --> 00:03:49,433
ne cherche pas d’aide,

77
00:03:49,433 --> 00:03:53,646
et pire, on ne tente rien en soi-mêmes.

78
00:03:53,646 --> 00:03:54,897
Méh.

79
00:03:56,065 --> 00:03:59,235
Le languissement n’est pas seulement insaisissable —

80
00:03:59,235 --> 00:04:02,571
C’est aussi difficile à évoquer en société.

81
00:04:02,571 --> 00:04:04,448
Lorsqu’on vous demande « Ça va ? »,

82
00:04:04,448 --> 00:04:06,659
on attend que vous répliquiez : « Génial ! »

83
00:04:06,659 --> 00:04:09,036
ou « Je vis mon rêve ! »

84
00:04:09,954 --> 00:04:12,123
C’est de la « positivité toxique ».

85
00:04:12,164 --> 00:04:14,292
(Rires)

86
00:04:16,919 --> 00:04:23,217
C’est la pression à rester perpétuellement optimiste.

87
00:04:23,259 --> 00:04:25,803
Si vous balbutiez « En fait, ça va juste… »,

88
00:04:25,845 --> 00:04:29,098
on vous demande de voir la lueur au bout du tunnel

89
00:04:29,140 --> 00:04:31,475
ou de compter vos bénédictions.

90
00:04:31,475 --> 00:04:32,935
Pas juste fastidieux.

91
00:04:32,935 --> 00:04:35,021
C’est mauvais conseil.

92
00:04:35,021 --> 00:04:36,772
Deux volontaires ?

93
00:04:37,690 --> 00:04:40,276
Je désignerai deux personnes si nécessaire, sans réticence.

94
00:04:40,276 --> 00:04:41,902
D’accord, par ici.

95
00:04:41,902 --> 00:04:43,195
Approchez le micro

96
00:04:43,195 --> 00:04:46,991
et cherchons un autre candidat

61
